topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut with a finality that echoed in the sudden silence of the office. I stood there, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white against the worn leather of my briefcase. He was gone. Just… gone. The framed commendations on his desk seemed to mock me, gleaming under the harsh fluorescent lights. I didn't move to collect my things; the inertia was too strong. A low, guttural sound escaped my throat, a sound that felt more like a physical expulsion than a vocalization. I stared at the empty chair where he used to sit, the scent of old paper and expensive cologne suddenly suffocating. "Well," I managed, the word scraping out dryly, "that’s that, then." I turned, the sound of my own footsteps sounding loud and clumsy on the polished floor, and walked out into the hallway, leaving the weight of his absence settling heavily on my shoulders.

[story 2]

I watched the silhouette of his car disappear around the corner, a fleeting smudge against the twilight. My jaw tightened until a sharp ache radiated behind my molars. It wasn't the shock that truly unsettled me; it was the sheer, unaddressed disappointment churning in my gut. I picked up the heavy, unmarked folder from his desk, the paper feeling flimsy and insignificant in my grasp. Every meticulously planned project we’d shared, every late night spent dissecting strategy, now felt like debris, scattered and worthless. I didn't bother to file it away. Instead, I let the folder slip from my fingers, letting it fall to the carpet with a soft, damning thud. I walked toward the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to find a reflection that matched the hollow feeling inside.

[story 3]

The silence in the study was deafening, a vacuum where his usual counsel and sharp critiques used to reside. I paced the perimeter of the room three times, my movements jerky and agitated. My breath hitched in shallow, rapid bursts. I stopped by the bookshelf, running a fingertip over the spines of the books—a collection of wisdom I had relied on, now seemingly abandoned. A coldness seeped into my extremities, a sensation that had nothing to do with the room's temperature. I didn't look at the door; I didn't need to. The knowledge that he had simply walked away, leaving me adrift in the aftermath of his mentorship, settled over me like a shroud, heavy and suffocating. I finally sank into the leather armchair, the fabric yielding beneath me with a sigh that mirrored my own profound deflation.
